summary: Atwood reflects on his journey from self-taught BASIC programmer to the .NET ecosystem, where he encountered formal development patterns for the first time. While acknowledging the benefits of patterns and practices, he warns against 'architecture astronauts' - developers who become so obsessed with abstraction and methodology that they lose sight of building actual working software. He draws a sharp distinction between 'doers' who ship real products used by real people, and 'talkers' who get lost in UML diagrams and theoretical frameworks. The post argues that patterns should always serve the practical goal of solving user problems, not become ends in themselves.
key_points: The .NET platform brought VB developers into mainstream development practices, exposing them to patterns absorbed from the Java world
'Architecture astronauts' are developers who work at such high levels of abstraction that they lose touch with practical implementation
The distinction between productive developers and astronauts lies in proving ideas through working applications used by actual users
Companies focused on doing (like Adobe) succeed and generate revenue, while companies focused on talking (like ObjectSpace) go bankrupt
Patterns and practices are valuable only when framed in the context of solving real user problems
money_quotes: When you go too far up, abstraction-wise, you run out of oxygen.
It's very hard to get them to write code or design programs, because they won't stop thinking about Architecture.
Software isn't about methodologies, languages, or even operating systems. It is about working applications.
A UML diagram can't push 500 pages per minute through a RIP.
There are two types of people in this industry. Talkers and Doers.
Patterns and practices are certainly good things, but they should always be framed in the context of a problem you're solving for the users.
key_insight: Software development patterns and architecture are only valuable when they serve the ultimate goal of building working applications that solve real problems for actual users.
